title: Magnetononlinear Hall effect from multigap topology in metal-organic frameworks
authors: Chun Wang Chau, Wojciech J. Jankowski, Bo Peng, Robert-Jan Slager
abstract: We unveil that non-Abelian multigap band topology characterized by nontrivial Euler class invariants induces observable magnetononlinear Hall transport phenomena. We demonstrate these effects in a highly-tunable class of recently synthesized two-dimensional kagome N-heterocyclic carbene (NHC) metal-organic frameworks. We showcase the controllability of the nonlinear effect upon applying external voltage, changing temperature, and chemical substitutions that preserve the bulk topology and associated edge states. Our findings therefore reveal an uncharted presence of Euler class topology in metal-organic materials that can be experimentally deduced through measurable magnetotransport.
summary: This paper explores the discovery of non-Abelian multigap topology in 2D metal-organic frameworks. By studying kagome-structured NHC-X materials, the authors show that the Euler class topology leads to a measurable magnetononlinear Hall effect. They demonstrate that this effect is highly tunable via chemical substitution, temperature, and voltage. This work establishes a new way to experimentally detect complex, non-Abelian topological invariants in organic-based materials.
main_problem: The study aims to identify and detect non-Abelian multigap band topology, specifically characterized by non-trivial Euler class invariants, using measurable transport phenomena.
main_result: The authors demonstrate that non-Abelian multigap topology induces an observable magnetononlinear Hall effect in 2D kagome metal-organic frameworks, providing a 'smoking-gun' probe for these topological states.
method: The researchers combined first-principles DFT calculations with Wannier-based transport simulations (WannierBerri, WannierTools) and analytical continuum k.p models.
model_or_system: The physical platform is a class of 2D kagome N-heterocyclic carbene (NHC) metal-organic frameworks (NHC-X, where X = Au, Ag, or Cu) featuring a three-band subspace near the Fermi level.
key_observables: Magnetononlinear Hall response (specifically the $\chi_{xy,z}^{orb}$ component), multi-gap edge states, and the Euler class invariant ($e_2$).
important_parameters: Chemical potential ($\mu$), temperature ($T$), external voltage, and chemical substitution (Au, Ag, Cu); regimes include the flat band limit and zero-temperature limit.
main_assumptions: The analytical derivation relies on a low-energy physics approximation via a $k \cdot p$ continuum model and focuses primarily on the zero-temperature limit.
figures_summary: Fig 1 shows the crystal structure, band structures, and edge states of the NHC-X kagome lattice. Fig 2 displays the nonlinear Hall response as a function of doping and temperature, including scaling behavior.
paper_structure: The paper introduces the material platform and topological features, presents DFT-based electronic structure and edge state analysis, derives the analytical magnetononlinear Hall response using a continuum model, and validates the results through numerical transport simulations.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: FIG. 1. Multigap topology in metal-organic frameworks. (a) Crystal structure of two-dimensional metal-organic NHC- Au kagome lattice framework, top view (above) and front view (below). The partial charge density of the kagome bands near the Fermi level is shown by the light cyan isosurface, indicating delocalized features across the kagome lattice, against the localized orbitals around metallic atoms. The hybridized states of the gold atoms and NHC ligands constitute the kagome basis orbitals A, B, C. (b) Comparison of band structure sections of kagome NHC-X (X = Au, Ag, Cu, AuH) metal-organic frameworks across hexagonal BZ, with quadratic band touching at the Γ point (BZ center), and...

FIG. 2. Magnetononlinear Hall effect from non-Abelian Euler band topology. (a) Orbital-projected kagome bands with e2 = 1 in NHC-Au metal-organic framework and nonlinear magnetoelectric Hall response at different dopings (µ). (b) Temperature dependence of the magnetononlinear Hall effect around the quadratic node in NHC-Au metal-organic frame- work. (c) Scaling of the magnetononlinear Hall response at temperature T = 1 meV in the NHC-X (X = Au, Ag, Cu) metal- organic frameworks, against the power-law dependence, χorb xy,z ∝|e2|/µ, arising from the multiband topology via non-Abelian Berry connection vorticity.
